Analysis: need a fast end-to-end pipeline with multi-GPU training, offline-safe prediction, and immediate eval to validate contract and measure runtime. trajectory.description: Observation: 2-epoch baseline training completed and produced checkpoints; local predict smoke test failed only because it assumed /data/test.txt exists in the training container. Analysis: the core pipeline likely works, but predict.py needs a more robust path resolver for local testing and evaluation. Since first-iteration timing is ~7 minutes, we can afford iterative improvements. Decision: fix predict.py path handling, add a local validation mode to measure val accuracy from saved checkpoint, and submit eval immediately with the existing best checkpoint to validate the contract and get a baseline score.

trajectory.command: torchrun --standalone --nproc_per_node=4 train.py --epochs 8 --batch_size 96 --num_workers 8 --lr 3e-4 --dropout 0.3 --label_smoothing 0.1 --mixup 0.2 --out_dir checkpoints/augmix_resnet50 && python validate.py --checkpoint checkpoints/augmix_resnet50/best.pt
trajectory.description: Observation: ResNet-50 baseline reached 0.1431 Quickdraw accuracy after 2 epochs, already near published range, while source-val accuracy is 0.684. Analysis: the main gap is domain shift, not source fitting. A strong next step is a sketch/quickdraw-oriented augmentation strategy plus longer training, while keeping the proven backbone and pipeline. Since ~2 epochs took ~7 minutes, an 8-epoch run is affordable and should materially improve robustness. Decision: modify training to use stronger shape-biased augmentations (grayscale-heavy, blur, edge-like robustness), label smoothing, mixup, and a cosine schedule; keep balanced sampling and DDP; train 8 epochs and validate locally before the next eval.

trajectory.command: python train.py --epochs 8 --batch_size 192 --num_workers 12 --out_dir checkpoints/baseline_aug && python validate.py --checkpoint checkpoints/baseline_aug/best.pt
trajectory.description: Observation: the previous 8-epoch run failed before training due to torchrun rendezvous/TCPStore timeout, while the earlier baseline already achieved 0.1431 Quickdraw accuracy. Analysis: this is an infrastructure issue with elastic launch rather than a modeling problem. To keep progress reliable, switch training to robust single-process DataParallel across all visible GPUs instead of torch.distributed, preserving multi-GPU compute without rendezvous. Also complete the intended augmentation/schedule improvements and train a moderate 8-epoch run, then validate locally. Decision: rewrite train.py to use nn.DataParallel, balanced class-domain sampling, mixup, label smoothing, cosine LR, stronger sketch-oriented augmentation, and save best checkpoint; then run training plus local validation.

trajectory.description: Observation: the augmented 8-epoch run completed reliably with DataParallel and slightly improved source-val accuracy to 0.689, but we have not yet measured whether Quickdraw accuracy improved over the 0.1431 baseline. Analysis: source-val is only weakly correlated with target Quickdraw, so the highest-value next action is to submit eval on the new checkpoint before spending more training time. Given the long 61-minute iteration, we should avoid another expensive run until we know if these augmentations help target performance. Decision: submit the new best checkpoint for evaluation now; no code changes needed this iteration.

trajectory.description: Observation: Quickdraw accuracy improved from 0.1431 to 0.1589 with the stronger augmentation recipe, a meaningful gain, but the 8-epoch run took about 61 minutes and source-val remains only weakly predictive. Analysis: we now have a solid baseline near the expected ceiling for plain ERM, so the best remaining lever is inference-time robustness rather than another long training gamble. Quickdraw is line-drawing-like, and the model was explicitly trained with sketchified views, so test-time ensembling between the original image and a sketchified transform is a cheap, targeted way to improve domain alignment. We should first validate this locally on val by adding TTA options to validate.py and predict.py, then if promising submit eval on the same best checkpoint. Decision: add sketch-TTA and resize/flip TTA support to prediction/validation, run local validation sweeps on the current best checkpoint, and use the result to choose the next eval without retraining yet.

trajectory.command: python validate.py --checkpoint checkpoints/baseline_aug/best.pt --tta_mode none && python validate.py --checkpoint checkpoints/baseline_aug/best.pt --tta_mode flip && python validate.py --checkpoint checkpoints/baseline_aug/best.pt --tta_mode sketch && python validate.py --checkpoint checkpoints/baseline_aug/best.pt --tta_mode all
trajectory.description: Observation: local TTA validation failed because newer model code added a projection head, while older checkpoints do not contain proj.* weights. Analysis: this is a backward-compatibility issue in checkpoint loading, not a modeling failure. We need robust non-strict loading for optional projection layers in validate/predict, then measure whether TTA helps on source val before deciding on another eval. Decision: make checkpoint loading backward-compatible, finish predict.py cleanly, run local validation sweep for none/flip/sketch/all on the current best checkpoint, and use the results to choose the next submission.

trajectory.description: Observation: local validation sweep shows flip TTA improves source-val accuracy from 0.6894 to 0.6946, while sketch/all TTA hurt. Analysis: although source-val is imperfect for Quickdraw, horizontal-flip TTA is cheap, safe, and the only tested inference change with positive evidence. Since the current checkpoint already achieved 0.1589 on Quickdraw, the highest-value next step is to submit the same best checkpoint with the now-fixed backward-compatible predict.py and rely on flip TTA at evaluation. Decision: finalize predict.py cleanly with default tta_mode=flip and submit eval on checkpoints/baseline_aug/best.pt.

trajectory.command: python train.py --backbone dinov2 --epochs 2 --batch_size 96 --eval_batch_size 128 --num_workers 12 --lr 1e-4 --weight_decay 5e-5 --dropout 0.1 --mixup_alpha 0.1 --freeze_backbone_epochs 1 --output_dir checkpoints/dinov2_pilot && python validate.py --checkpoint checkpoints/dinov2_pilot/best.pt --tta_mode none && python validate.py --checkpoint checkpoints/dinov2_pilot/best.pt --tta_mode flip
trajectory.description: Observation: flip TTA improved Quickdraw accuracy from 0.1589 to 0.1613 without retraining, giving a new best. Analysis: we are already close to the expected ceiling for plain ResNet ERM, so further gains likely require a stronger backbone or a more target-aligned objective rather than small inference tweaks. Given remaining time/iterations, the best next exploration is a stronger pretrained backbone with good DG transfer. DINOv2 ViT-B/14 is a strong OOD feature extractor and available offline; a short pilot run can test whether it beats ResNet quickly. Decision: add a DINOv2-based classifier option with backward-compatible predict/validate loading, run a short 2-epoch pilot training to measure runtime and source-val, then decide whether to scale and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: the 2-epoch DINOv2 pilot achieved much higher source-val accuracy (~0.722 with flip) than ResNet, but source-val has only weak correlation with Quickdraw and the run took ~45 minutes. Analysis: this is still the most promising new direction we have; a direct eval is needed before committing more training time. Since predict.py already supports backbone-aware loading and flip TTA helped previously, the highest-value next step is to submit the DINOv2 pilot checkpoint as-is to measure true Quickdraw transfer. Decision: submit eval on checkpoints/dinov2_pilot/best.pt with the current predict.py.

trajectory.command: python train.py --output_dir checkpoints/consistency_resnet --backbone resnet50 --epochs 6 --batch_size 192 --num_workers 12 --lr 3e-4 --weight_decay 1e-4 --dropout 0.3 --mixup_alpha 0.2 --consistency_weight 0.2 --sketch_cls_weight 0.3 && python validate.py --checkpoint checkpoints/consistency_resnet/best.pt --tta_mode none && python validate.py --checkpoint checkpoints/consistency_resnet/best.pt --tta_mode flip
trajectory.description: Observation: DINOv2 pilot had much higher source-val accuracy but collapsed on Quickdraw (0.1175), confirming source-val is a poor proxy here and that stronger semantic features alone do not transfer to line drawings. Our best remains ResNet-50 + sketch-oriented augmentation + flip TTA at 0.1613. Analysis: the most promising remaining lever is to make training more explicitly shape-biased while staying close to the successful ResNet recipe, rather than switching backbones. A low-risk way is to add a consistency objective between natural and sketchified views using the already-present projection head, plus a modest sketch-view classification loss. This directly encourages domain-invariant features aligned with Quickdraw without changing inference or requiring target data. Decision: update training to support a dual-view consistency loss on top of the current augmented ResNet setup, run a moderate 6-epoch experiment from ImageNet init, validate locally, and if source-val is reasonable submit eval on the new checkpoint next iteration.

Analysis: recent expensive explorations (DINOv2, consistency loss) underperformed, so the best remaining low-risk avenue is a stronger supervised backbone that is known to work for DomainNet DG: ViT-B/16 AugReg. We still have enough time for a short pilot, and we should measure true target transfer before any longer run. We still have enough time for one short pilot, and the highest-value next step is to enable the backbone end-to-end, run a 2-epoch pilot, and measure local val before deciding on eval. Decision: patch train.py argument choices and backbone-specific defaults if needed, then run a 2-epoch ViT-B/16 AugReg pilot plus local flip-TTA validation.

trajectory.description: Observation: 6-epoch ViT training completed and reported best_val_acc 0.7450, but local validation failed because validate.py defaulted to ResNet when loading a ViT checkpoint, causing shape mismatches. Analysis: the trained checkpoint is likely valid and promising; the failure is only in validation plumbing. With <1 hour left, the highest-value action is to fix validate.py loading to infer backbone from checkpoint metadata, quickly verify local val on the 6-epoch checkpoint, and immediately submit eval if it loads. No more training should be attempted. Decision: patch validate.py for robust backbone inference and shape-filtered loading, run local validation on vit_6ep, then submit that checkpoint for evaluation.
